items.q: How are phobias MAINTAINED according to Mowrer's two-process model?
items.correct: Through operant conditioning - avoidance reduces anxiety (negative reinforcement).
items.misconception: Through classical conditioning continuing over time.
items.why: Classical conditioning ACQUIRES the phobia; operant conditioning MAINTAINS it. Students conflate these. Avoiding the feared object reduces anxiety, reinforcing the avoidance.

items.q: What is the difference between reliability and validity?
items.correct: Reliability is consistency; validity is whether it measures what it claims to.
items.misconception: They are essentially the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Reliability is for quantitative, validity for qualitative.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms. A bathroom scale that adds 5kg every time is reliable (consistent) but not valid (wrong reading). You can be reliable without being valid.

items.q: According to Maslow, when can self-actualisation be achieved?
items.correct: Only after lower deficiency needs are largely met.
items.misconception: Anyone can self-actualise at any time through willpower.
items.why: Students miss the hierarchical structure. You cannot pursue growth needs while physiological, safety, love, and esteem needs are unmet - that's the whole point of a hierarchy.

items.q: Damage to which area mainly explains HM's amnesia?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Hippocampus. “Frontal lobe” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Phineas Gage's case mainly illustrates the role of the:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Frontal lobe. “Temporal lobe” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Phineas Gage's accident take place?
items.why: Exact answer: 1848. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
